questions.summary: Identify ion with different electron count
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Count the electrons in each ion by taking the atomic number (proton number) and adjusting for the charge. Three ions will have the same electron count; one will differ.
steps: Step 1 – Find electrons in each ion (electrons = protons − charge):
A: Ga³⁺ → Ga has 31 protons, lose 3 electrons → 31 − 3 = 28 electrons
B: Cl⁻ → Cl has 17 protons, gain 1 electron → 17 + 1 = 18 electrons
C: S²⁻ → S has 16 protons, gain 2 electrons → 16 + 2 = 18 electrons
D: Ca²⁺ → Ca has 20 protons, lose 2 electrons → 20 − 2 = 18 electrons
Step 2 – Compare: Cl⁻, S²⁻, and Ca²⁺ all have 18 electrons. Ga³⁺ has 28 electrons — the odd one out.
Answer: A

questions.summary: Determine empirical formula from percentage composition
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: To find the empirical formula, divide each element's percentage by its relative atomic mass to get molar ratios, then simplify to the smallest whole number ratio.
steps: Step 3 – Ratio C:H:O = 2:5:1, so empirical formula = C₂H₅O
Answer: D

questions.summary: Greatest CO2 volume from hydrocarbon combustion
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Each alkane CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ produces n moles of CO₂ per mole burned. Multiply moles of hydrocarbon by the number of carbon atoms (n) to find moles of CO₂ produced. At same T and P, volume is proportional to moles of CO₂.
steps: Rule: moles CO₂ = moles of hydrocarbon × number of carbon atoms (n)
A: 0.4 mol C₂H₆ → 0.4 × 2 = 0.8 mol CO₂
B: 0.3 mol C₃H₈ → 0.3 × 3 = 0.9 mol CO₂
C: 0.2 mol C₄H₁₀ → 0.2 × 4 = 0.8 mol CO₂
D: 0.1 mol C₅H₁₂ → 0.1 × 5 = 0.5 mol CO₂
Answer: B (0.9 mol CO₂ is the greatest)

questions.summary: Smallest atom economy for BaCl2 production
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Atom economy = (Mr of desired product / total Mr of all products) × 100. The smallest atom economy means the most 'wasted' mass in by-products, so we want the reaction where BaCl₂ makes up the smallest fraction of total product mass.
steps: Formula: Atom economy = (Mr of BaCl₂ / sum of Mr of ALL products) × 100. Mr of BaCl₂ = 137 + 71 = 208.
A: BaCl₂·2H₂O → BaCl₂ + 2H₂O. Products: BaCl₂ (208) + 2H₂O (36). Total = 244. AE = 208/244 × 100 = 85.2%
B: BaO + 2HCl → BaCl₂ + H₂O. Products: BaCl₂ (208) + H₂O (18). Total = 226. AE = 208/226 × 100 = 92.0%
C: BaCO₃ + 2HCl → BaCl₂ + CO₂ + H₂O. Products: BaCl₂ (208) + CO₂ (44) + H₂O (18). Total = 270. AE = 208/270 × 100 = 77.0%
D: Ba + 2HCl → BaCl₂ + H₂. Products: BaCl₂ (208) + H₂ (2). Total = 210. AE = 208/210 × 100 = 99.0%
Answer: C — it has the smallest atom economy (77.0%) because two by-products (CO₂ and H₂O) are formed, giving the largest total by-product mass.

questions.summary: Calculate percentage uncertainty from titration readings
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: When subtracting two readings, their absolute uncertainties add together. The percentage uncertainty is then calculated using (total absolute uncertainty ÷ titre value) × 100.
steps: Step 1 – Find the titre: 24.95 − 5.00 = 19.95 cm³
Step 2 – Find the total absolute uncertainty: each reading has ±0.05 cm³, so total = 0.05 + 0.05 = ±0.10 cm³
Step 3 – Calculate percentage uncertainty: (0.10 ÷ 19.95) × 100 = 0.501% ≈ 0.50%
Answer: D (0.50%)

questions.summary: Element with most singly occupied orbitals
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Singly occupied orbitals are orbitals containing exactly one electron (unpaired). Apply Hund's rule when filling subshells to count them for each element.
steps: Step 1 – Write electron configurations and identify singly occupied orbitals using Hund's rule:
A) C (Z=6): 1s² 2s² 2p² → the two 2p electrons occupy separate 2p orbitals → 2 singly occupied orbitals
B) Cl (Z=17): 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁵ → five 3p electrons fill as ↑↓ ↑↓ ↑ → 1 singly occupied orbital
C) Ca (Z=20): 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² → all orbitals fully paired → 0 singly occupied orbitals
D) Ga (Z=31): [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s² 4p¹ → one 4p electron → 1 singly occupied orbital
Step 2 – Compare: C=2, Cl=1, Ca=0, Ga=1 → Carbon (C) has the greatest number of singly occupied orbitals.
Answer: A

questions.summary: Identify compound with polar molecules
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A molecule is polar overall if it has polar bonds AND an asymmetric shape so the bond dipoles do NOT cancel. OCl₂ has a bent shape (like water, with a lone pair on O), so its two O–Cl dipoles do not cancel, giving a net dipole moment.
steps: Step 1 – Check each molecule for polar bonds: All four compounds contain bonds between atoms of different electronegativities (O–Cl, B–Cl, C–Cl, S–Cl), so all have polar bonds.

Step 2 – Check molecular geometry to see if dipoles cancel: BCl₃ is trigonal planar → dipoles cancel → non-polar. CCl₄ is tetrahedral → dipoles cancel → non-polar. SCl₆ is octahedral → dipoles cancel → non-polar.
Step 3 – OCl₂: oxygen has 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs → bent/V-shaped geometry (like H₂O). The two O–Cl bond dipoles point in similar directions and do NOT cancel → net dipole → polar molecule.
Answer: A (OCl₂)

questions.summary: Element with highest melting point in Period 3
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Silicon has a giant covalent (macromolecular) structure requiring many strong covalent bonds to be broken on melting, giving it a very high melting point (~1414°C). The other options (P, S, Cl) are simple molecular substances with weak van der Waals forces between molecules, so they have much lower melting points.
steps: Step 1: Identify the structure of each element. Silicon = giant covalent lattice; phosphorus (P₄), sulfur (S₈), and chlorine (Cl₂) = simple molecular structures.

Step 2: Giant covalent structures require breaking many strong covalent bonds to melt → very high melting points. Simple molecular substances only need to overcome weak London dispersion (van der Waals) forces → low melting points.
Step 3: Compare approximate melting points: Si ≈ 1414°C, S ≈ 113°C, P ≈ 44°C, Cl₂ ≈ −101°C.
Answer: A — silicon

questions.summary: Explain boiling point trend down halogens group
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Halogens are simple molecular substances with only London (induced dipole-dipole) forces between molecules. Going down the group, molecules get larger with more electrons, so polarisability increases, leading to stronger London forces and higher boiling points.
steps: Step 1: Identify the intermolecular forces in halogens. Halogens (F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂) are non-polar molecules, so the ONLY intermolecular forces are London forces (induced dipole-dipole). This rules out B (hydrogen bonds) and C (permanent dipole-dipole).
Step 2: Rule out A — covalent bonds are intramolecular (within the molecule) and breaking them is not relevant to boiling point trends between molecules.
Step 3: Going down Group 17, each halogen has more electrons and a larger electron cloud. A larger, more polarisable electron cloud means stronger, more frequent London forces between molecules.
Step 4: Stronger London forces require more energy to overcome → higher boiling point down the group.
Answer: D

questions.summary: Silver compound insoluble in concentrated ammonia
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Silver halides have different solubilities in concentrated NH₃(aq): AgCl dissolves readily, AgBr dissolves in concentrated NH₃, but AgI is insoluble even in concentrated NH₃ because its lattice energy is too high for the complex [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ to form.
steps: Step 1: Recall that NH₃(aq) dissolves silver compounds by forming the diamminesilver(I) complex ion: Ag⁺ + 2NH₃ → [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺
Step 2: AgNO₃ is soluble in water anyway, so it dissolves easily.
Step 3: AgCl (white precipitate) dissolves in dilute NH₃(aq) — soluble in NH₃.
Step 4: AgBr (cream precipitate) dissolves only in concentrated NH₃(aq) — soluble in concentrated NH₃.
Step 5: AgI (yellow precipitate) does NOT dissolve even in concentrated NH₃(aq) — insoluble.
Answer: D — AgI

questions.summary: Temperature rise in neutralisation with half volumes
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: The temperature rise in a neutralisation depends on the moles of water formed AND the total volume being heated. Halving the volumes halves both the heat produced and the mass of solution, so these effects cancel out — the temperature rise stays the same.
steps: Step 1: Calculate moles of NaOH (and HNO₃) in experiment 1: moles = 1.00 mol dm⁻³ × 0.0500 dm³ = 0.0500 mol
Step 2: Heat produced in experiment 1: q₁ = mc∆T ∝ (total volume) × ∆T = 100.0 cm³ × 6.0 °C
Step 3: In experiment 2, moles = 1.00 × 0.0250 = 0.0250 mol — half the moles, so half the heat produced. But total volume is also halved: 50.0 cm³.
Step 4: ∆T₂ = q₂ / (m₂ × c) = (½q₁) / (½m₁ × c) = q₁ / (m₁ × c) = 6.0 °C
Answer: C — 6.0 °C

questions.summary: Calculate enthalpy of formation using combustion data
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Use Hess's Law: ΔH_reaction = Σ ΔcH(reactants) − Σ ΔcH(products). The enthalpy of formation route uses combustion data: sum of combustion enthalpies of reactants minus combustion enthalpy of product.
steps: Step 1 – Apply Hess's Law using combustion data: ΔH_rxn = [sum of ΔcH of reactants] − [ΔcH of product]
Step 2 – Identify reactants and their moles: 4C(s) and 5H₂(g), product: C₄H₁₀(g)
Step 3 – Calculate sum of combustion enthalpies of reactants: 4 × (−393.5) + 5 × (−285.8) = −1574.0 + (−1429.0) = −3003.0 kJ mol⁻¹
Step 4 – Subtract combustion enthalpy of product: ΔH_rxn = −3003.0 − (−2876.5) = −3003.0 + 2876.5 = −126.5 kJ mol⁻¹
Answer: B, −126.5 kJ mol⁻¹

questions.summary: Condition shifting equilibrium towards products
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Use Le Chatelier's principle: the system will shift to oppose any change. For temperature, a decrease favours the exothermic direction (forward reaction, products), since ΔH = −9.4 kJ mol⁻¹. For pressure, moles of gas are equal on both sides (1+1=2 on left, 2 on right), so pressure has no effect on equilibrium position.
steps: Step 1 – Check moles of gas each side: Reactants: H₂ + I₂ = 2 moles gas. Products: 2HI = 2 moles gas. Equal moles → pressure changes have NO effect on equilibrium position. Eliminates A and C.

Step 2 – The reaction is EXOTHERMIC (ΔH = −9.4 kJ mol⁻¹, negative). By Le Chatelier's principle, DECREASING temperature favours the exothermic (forward) direction, shifting equilibrium towards products.
Step 3 – INCREASING temperature favours the endothermic (reverse) direction → shifts away from products. Eliminates D.
Answer: B — decrease the temperature

questions.summary: Systematic name of bromobutene stereoisomer
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: The structure shows a 4-carbon chain with a double bond at C2 and a Br substituent at C2. For E/Z nomenclature, assign priorities on each carbon of the double bond: on C2, Br > CH3; on C3, CH3 > H. The higher priority groups (Br on C2 and CH3 on C3) are on opposite sides of the double bond, making it E.
steps: Step 1 – Identify the parent chain: 4 carbons with a C=C at position 2 → but-2-ene.
Step 2 – Identify the substituent: Br is on C2 → 2-bromobut-2-ene.
Step 3 – Assign E/Z: On C2, priorities are Br (atomic number 35) > CH3 (C, at. no. 6). On C3, priorities are CH3 > H.
Step 4 – Check the geometry: From the skeletal structure, the Br (on C2, going down-left) and the CH3/ethyl group (on C3, going right) are on OPPOSITE sides of the double bond → E configuration.
Answer: A — E-2-bromobut-2-ene

questions.summary: Molecular formula from skeletal formula
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: In a skeletal formula, each line-end/junction is a carbon, and you add enough H atoms to give each carbon 4 bonds, then count the explicit O atoms (C=O and O-H).
steps: Step 1 – Count carbons: Trace every line end and junction in the skeleton. The structure has a ketone (C=O) carbon plus an isopropyl group on one side and a methylpropyl chain on the other. Counting all line ends/junctions gives 6 carbons total.
Step 2 – Count oxygens: There is one C=O (ketone) oxygen and one O-H (alcohol) group → 2 oxygens total.

Step 3 – Count hydrogens: Use the general formula. With 6 carbons, 2 oxygens, no rings or extra double bonds beyond the C=O (one degree of unsaturation), the saturated formula would be CₙH₂ₙ₊₂O₂ = C₆H₁₄O₂. One C=O means one degree of unsaturation, so subtract 2H → C₆H₁₂O₂. Verify by adding H's to each carbon individually: the six carbons give CH₃–C(=O)–C(CH₃)(OH)–CH(CH₃)₂ expanded = 3+0+0+1+3+1+3+1 = 12 H atoms. ✓
Answer: C – C₆H₁₂O₂

questions.summary: Number of structural isomers of C5H12
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Structural isomers of C₅H₁₂ are alkanes (saturated, no rings). Systematically draw all possible carbon skeleton arrangements to count distinct structures.
steps: Step 1: C₅H₁₂ fits the alkane formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, so these are all saturated alkanes (pentane isomers).
Step 2: Draw straight chain — pentane: C-C-C-C-C (1 structure)
Step 3: Draw 4-carbon chain with one methyl branch — methylbutane (isopentane): C-C(CH₃)-C-C (branch on C2 or C3, but these are the same by symmetry → 1 structure)
Step 4: Draw 3-carbon chain with two methyl branches — dimethylpropane (neopentane): C(CH₃)₄ central carbon with 4 methyl groups → 1 structure
Step 5: Total = 3 structural isomers
Answer: B (3)

questions.summary: Organic compound with lowest boiling point
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: All four compounds are C8H18 isomers (octane). More branched molecules have a more compact/spherical shape, reducing the surface area available for London dispersion forces, lowering the boiling point. 2,3,4-trimethylpentane is the most highly branched isomer, so it has the weakest intermolecular forces and lowest boiling point.
steps: Step 1: Count carbons in each compound — A: 2,3,4-trimethylpentane = 5+3 = C8; B: 2,3-dimethylhexane = 6+2 = C8; C: 2-methylheptane = 7+1 = C8; D: octane = C8. All are C8H18 isomers, so Mr is the same.

Step 2: Since Mr is identical, the only factor affecting boiling point is the degree of branching. More branching → more compact shape → less surface area → weaker London dispersion forces → lower boiling point.
Step 3: Rank branching — A (three methyl branches on pentane backbone) is the most branched; D (straight chain) is the least branched.
Answer: A — 2,3,4-trimethylpentane has the most branching and therefore the lowest boiling point.

questions.summary: Alcohol forming E/Z stereoisomers on dehydration
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Acid-catalysed dehydration of an alcohol forms an alkene. For E/Z stereoisomers to exist, each carbon of the C=C double bond must have two DIFFERENT substituents attached to it.
steps: Step 1 – Recall the reaction: alcohol + acid catalyst → alkene + water (elimination). E/Z isomerism requires each C of the C=C to bear two different groups.
Step 2 – Check each option by drawing the alkene product:
A: pentan-3-ol → pent-2-ene (CH3CH=CHCH2CH3). C2 has: H and CH3. C3 has: H and CH2CH3. Both carbons have two DIFFERENT groups → E/Z isomers ARE possible. ✓
B: pentan-1-ol → pent-1-ene (CH2=CHCH2CH2CH3). C1 has two H atoms → both substituents on C1 are the same → NO E/Z isomerism. ✗
C: 2-methylbutan-2-ol → 2-methylbut-2-ene (CH3C(CH3)=CHCH3) or methylbut-1-ene. The trisubstituted alkene CH3C(CH3)=CHCH3 has C2 bearing two CH3 groups (same) → NO E/Z isomerism. ✗
D: 2,2-dimethylpropan-1-ol → 2-methylbut-1-ene type product; C1 has two H atoms → NO E/Z isomerism. ✗
Answer: A

questions.summary: Identify alcohol from IR spectrum of oxidation product
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: When heated under reflux with acidified K₂Cr₂O₇, primary and secondary alcohols are oxidised (primary → carboxylic acid, secondary → ketone), but tertiary alcohols cannot be oxidised. The IR spectrum shows NO broad O-H absorption around 2500–3300 cm⁻¹ (carboxylic acid) and NO broad O-H around 3200–3550 cm⁻¹ (alcohol), but does show a C=O peak around 1700 cm⁻¹, indicating a ketone product. This means the alcohol must be a secondary alcohol (oxidised to a ketone).
steps: Step 1: Analyse the IR spectrum. There is a strong absorption around 1700 cm⁻¹ (C=O stretch) but NO broad O-H peak. This indicates the product is a ketone (not a carboxylic acid, not an alcohol).

Step 2: Determine what type of alcohol gives a ketone on oxidation with acidified K₂Cr₂O₇. Secondary alcohols → ketones. Primary alcohols → aldehydes → carboxylic acids (under reflux). Tertiary alcohols → not oxidised.

Step 3: Identify which option is a secondary alcohol. A = tertiary alcohol (OH on carbon bonded to 3 carbons). B = secondary alcohol (OH on carbon bonded to 2 carbons). C = primary alcohol (OH on end carbon). D = primary alcohol (OH on end carbon).
Answer: B — it is a secondary alcohol, which is oxidised to a ketone by acidified K₂Cr₂O₇ under reflux, consistent with the IR spectrum showing a C=O peak and no O-H peak.

questions.summary: Alcohol unlikely to have fragment ion at m/z=43
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A fragment at m/z = 43 corresponds to either CH₃CO⁺ (43) or C₃H₇⁺ (43). For alcohols, common fragments come from C-C bond cleavage adjacent to the OH group. We need to find which molecule cannot produce a fragment of mass 43.
steps: Step 1: Identify what m/z = 43 means. C₃H₇⁺ has mass = 3(12) + 7(1) = 43. So a propyl fragment (CH₃CH₂CH₂⁺ or (CH₃)₂CH⁺) would give m/z = 43.

Step 2: Check option A — CH₃CH₂CH(OH)CH₃ (butan-2-ol). Cleavage between C1-C2 gives CH₃CH₂⁺ (m/z = 29) and [CH(OH)CH₃]⁺ (m/z = 45). Cleavage between C3-C4 gives CH₃⁺ (m/z = 15) and [CH₃CH₂CH(OH)]⁺ (m/z = 59). No C₃H₇⁺ fragment at 43 is produced from this molecule.
Step 3: Check option B — CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂OH (butan-1-ol). Cleavage between C3-C4 gives CH₃CH₂CH₂⁺ (m/z = 43). ✓ Fragment at 43 exists.
Step 4: Check option C — CH₃CH(OH)CH₂CH₂CH₃ (pentan-2-ol). Cleavage between C3-C4 gives CH₃CH₂CH₂⁺ (m/z = 43). ✓ Fragment at 43 exists.
Step 5: Check option D — (CH₃)₂CHCH₂OH (2-methylpropan-1-ol). Cleavage between C1-C2 gives (CH₃)₂CH⁺ (m/z = 43). ✓ Fragment at 43 exists.
Answer: A — CH₃CH₂CH(OH)CH₃ cannot produce a fragment ion at m/z = 43.

questions.summary: Reactions and polymerisation of 2-chloropropene
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: 2-chloropropene has a C=C double bond, so it undergoes electrophilic addition with Br₂ and H₂/Ni, and acid-catalysed hydration with steam. The major product with steam follows Markovnikov's rule — OH adds to the more substituted carbon (C2, which already bears the Cl).
steps: Step 1 — Reaction with Br₂ (left box): Br₂ adds across the C=C double bond (electrophilic addition). Each bromine atom adds to one carbon. Product: CH₃CBrClCH₂Br — i.e., H₃C–C(Cl)(Br)–CH₂Br. Draw: H₃C–C(Cl)(Br)–CH(Br)–H (1-bromo-2-bromo-2-chloropropane).

Step 2 — Reaction with H₂/Ni (right box): H₂ adds across the C=C (hydrogenation/electrophilic addition with H₂ and Ni catalyst). Product: CH₃CHClCH₃ — i.e., H₃C–C(Cl)(H)–CH₂H = 2-chloropropane. Draw: H₃C–C(Cl)(H)–CH₂–H.

Step 3 — Reaction with steam (bottom box, major product): Steam (H₂O) adds across the C=C by acid-catalysed electrophilic addition. By Markovnikov's rule, OH adds to the more substituted carbon (C2, already bearing Cl) and H adds to C3. Major product: H₃C–C(Cl)(OH)–CH₂–H = 2-chloro-2-propanol (CH₃C(Cl)(OH)CH₃ is not right since C3 has 2H — actually the structure is H₃C–C(Cl)(OH)–CH₂H, i.e., CH₃CCl(OH)CH₃).
Step 4 — Part (ii) catalyst for steam reaction: The catalyst is an acid. Acceptable answers: H⁺ / H₂SO₄ / H₃PO₄ / HCl.

Answer (i): Left box = CH₃CBrClCH₂Br (addition of Br₂ across double bond); Right box = CH₃CHClCH₃ (addition of H₂); Bottom box = CH₃C(OH)ClCH₃ — wait, checking: 2-chloropropene is CH₃–C(Cl)=CH₂, so C2 has Cl and C3 has 2H. Markovnikov: OH to C2, H to C3 → CH₃–C(Cl)(OH)–CH₃. Answer (ii): H⁺ / H₂SO₄ / H₃PO₄

questions.summary: Mass spectrometry, ionic compounds and ionisation energies
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Magnesium has atomic number 12 (always 12 protons). The m/z value for a 1+ ion equals the mass number. Neutrons = mass number − protons. A 1+ ion has lost 1 electron, so electrons = protons − 1 = 11. For part (ii), Ar = Σ(isotope mass × abundance)/100.
steps: Part (i): Mg has atomic number 12, so ALWAYS 12 protons. As a 1+ ion, it has lost 1 electron → 11 electrons. Neutrons = mass number − 12.
m/z = 24: protons = 12, neutrons = 24 − 12 = 12, electrons = 11
m/z = 25: protons = 12, neutrons = 25 − 12 = 13, electrons = 11
m/z = 26: protons = 12, neutrons = 26 − 12 = 14, electrons = 11
Part (ii): Formula: Ar = (m/z × % abundance summed) ÷ 100
Answer: Ar = 24.32

questions.summary: Methanol production, catalyst, Boltzmann and Kc
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy (Ea). Using the Boltzmann distribution, this means a greater proportion of molecules have energy ≥ the new lower Ea, so more successful collisions occur per unit time, increasing the rate.
steps: Step 1 – Label the axes: x-axis = 'kinetic energy (of molecules)', y-axis = 'number of molecules (with that energy)' or 'probability/frequency'.

Step 2 – Draw the Boltzmann distribution curve: starts at the origin, rises to a peak, then tails off to the right (never touching the x-axis). The curve must NOT touch the y-axis at zero energy and must tail off asymptotically.

Step 3 – Mark TWO activation energies on the x-axis: Ea (without catalyst) further to the right, and Ea' (with catalyst) closer to the peak, clearly labelled. The catalysed Ea must be lower (further left).
Step 4 – Written explanation point 1: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a LOWER activation energy.

Step 5 – Written explanation point 2: With the lower Ea, a greater proportion/number of molecules have energy greater than or equal to this new activation energy (shown by the larger shaded area under the curve to the right of Ea').
Step 6 – Written explanation point 3: Therefore there are more successful collisions per unit time, so the rate of reaction increases.

Answer: The catalyst lowers Ea → larger fraction of molecules exceed Ea → more successful collisions per unit time → faster rate. [4 marks: axes labelled + correct curve + two Ea values marked + explanation linking lower Ea to more molecules with sufficient energy]

questions.summary: Ethanoic acid reactions, redox and titration with Ba(OH)2
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: An acid is a proton (H⁺) donor; a weak acid only partially dissociates in solution. For balancing, count atoms on each side and adjust coefficients. For redox, assign oxidation numbers to identify which element loses electrons (oxidised) and which gains electrons (reduced).
steps: Part (a): An acid releases H⁺ ions (proton donor). A weak acid partially dissociates/ionises in solution (not fully).

Part (b)(i) – Balancing: On the right, (CH₃COO)₃Al contains 3 CH₃COO groups and 1 Al. Start by balancing Al: need 2 Al on left to match 2 formula units of (CH₃COO)₃Al on right. That needs 6 CH₃COOH on left. H₂ produced: 6 H from the acid → 3 H₂. Check: 2 Al | 6 CH₃COOH → 2 (CH₃COO)₃Al | 3 H₂ ✓
Answer (b)(i): 2 Al(s) + 6 CH₃COOH(aq) → 2 (CH₃COO)₃Al(aq) + 3 H₂(g)
Part (b)(ii) – Oxidation numbers: Al in its elemental form = 0; in (CH₃COO)₃Al, Al is +3. So Al goes from 0 to +3 (loses electrons = oxidised).
H in CH₃COOH (acid): the acidic H has oxidation number +1; in H₂(g), H = 0. So H goes from +1 to 0 (gains electrons = reduced).
Answer (b)(ii): Element oxidised = Aluminium (Al), oxidation number change from 0 to +3. Element reduced = Hydrogen (H), oxidation number change from +1 to 0.

questions.summary: Combustion enthalpy, bond enthalpies and molar mass of alkene
questions.msPaper: June 2017 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: For part (i), a reaction is exothermic when more energy is released forming bonds than is required to break bonds. For part (ii), use ΔH = bonds broken − bonds formed, then rearrange to find the unknown C=C bond enthalpy.
steps: Part (a)(i): More energy is released when forming bonds in the products than is required to break bonds in the reactants. (Bond breaking is endothermic; bond making is exothermic — and the making releases more energy overall.)
Part (a)(ii) — Identify bonds broken in reactants: C₂H₄ has 1× C=C, 4× C–H; 3O₂ has 3× O=O. Let C=C = x (unknown).
Energy to break bonds = x + (4 × 413) + (3 × 498) = x + 1652 + 1494 = x + 3146 kJ mol⁻¹
Identify bonds formed in products: 2CO₂ has 4× C=O; 2H₂O has 4× O–H.
Energy released forming bonds = (4 × 805) + (4 × 464) = 3220 + 1856 = 5076 kJ mol⁻¹
Use: ΔH = bonds broken − bonds formed → −1318 = (x + 3146) − 5076
Rearrange: x = −1318 − 3146 + 5076 = +612 kJ mol⁻¹
Answer: Average bond enthalpy of C=C = +612 kJ mol⁻¹

questions.summary: Electron config of X, formula of sodium compound
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Element X has configuration 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁴, giving it 16 electrons — this is sulfur (Group 6), which needs 2 more electrons to complete its outer shell, so it forms a 2− ion. Sodium (Group 1) forms a 1+ ion, so two Na⁺ ions are needed to balance one X²⁻ ion.
steps: Step 1: Count electrons in X: 2+2+6+2+4 = 16 electrons → element is sulfur, in Group 6.
Step 2: Group 6 elements gain 2 electrons to achieve a full outer shell → X forms a 2− ion (X²⁻).
Step 3: Sodium is in Group 1 → Na forms a 1+ ion (Na⁺).
Step 4: To balance charges: need 2 × Na⁺ to cancel 1 × X²⁻ → formula is Na₂X.
Answer: C — Na₂X

questions.summary: Number of oxygen atoms in 88g CO2
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Find moles of CO₂, then moles of O atoms (×2 per molecule), then multiply by Avogadro's number to get total oxygen atoms.
steps: Step 1 – Molar mass of CO₂: 12 + (2×16) = 44 g/mol
Step 2 – Moles of CO₂: n = mass/Mr = 88.0/44 = 2.00 mol
Step 3 – Each CO₂ contains 2 oxygen atoms, so moles of O atoms = 2.00 × 2 = 4.00 mol
Step 4 – Number of O atoms = 4.00 × 6.02×10²³ = 2.41×10²⁴
Answer: C (2.41 × 10²⁴)

questions.summary: Identify compound from percentage composition by mass
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: To find the empirical formula, divide each percentage by the element's relative atomic mass to get molar ratios, then simplify. The answer is NH₄NO₃ (B).
steps: Step 3 – Multiply all by 2 to get whole numbers: H: 4, N: 2, O: 3 → empirical formula H₄N₂O₃
Step 4 – H₄N₂O₃ is the same as NH₄NO₃ (ammonium nitrate), which matches option B.
Answer: B

questions.summary: Mass of sodium reacting to produce hydrogen gas
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Convert the volume of H₂ to moles using molar volume at RTP (24,000 cm³/mol), then use the mole ratio from the equation (2Na : 1H₂) to find moles of Na, and finally calculate mass.
steps: Step 1 – Moles of H₂: n = V ÷ 24000 = 960 ÷ 24000 = 0.04 mol
Step 2 – Mole ratio from equation: 2Na : 1H₂, so moles of Na = 2 × 0.04 = 0.08 mol
Step 3 – Mass of Na: mass = n × Mᵣ = 0.08 × 23 = 1.84 g
Answer: C (1.84 g)

questions.summary: Identify equation not representing neutralisation
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A neutralisation reaction is specifically defined as an acid reacting with a base (or alkali) to form a salt and water only. Reaction A is a metal reacting with an acid — this is a displacement/redox reaction, not neutralisation, as it produces H₂ gas and no base is involved.
steps: Step 1: Recall the definition — neutralisation = acid + base → salt + water (no other products, and must involve a base neutralising an acid).

Step 2: Check each option — B: NH₃ (base) + H₂SO₄ (acid) → salt only ✓ neutralisation. C: Na₂CO₃ (base/carbonate) + CH₃COOH (acid) → salt + CO₂ + H₂O ✓ neutralisation (carbonate reacting with acid). D: CuO (base) + HNO₃ (acid) → salt + water ✓ neutralisation.

Step 3: Option A — Zn (a metal, not a base) + HCl (acid) → ZnCl₂ + H₂. This is a metal-acid reaction (redox/displacement). No base is involved and H₂ is produced, so it is NOT a neutralisation reaction.
Answer: A

questions.summary: Oxidation number of Fe in K2FeO4
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: In a neutral compound, all oxidation numbers must sum to zero. K is always +1 and O is always -2, so you can solve for Fe algebraically.
steps: Step 1 – Write the rule: sum of all oxidation numbers in a neutral compound = 0
Step 2 – Assign known oxidation numbers: K = +1 (×2 atoms = +2 total); O = −2 (×4 atoms = −8 total)
Step 3 – Set up equation: 2(+1) + Fe + 4(−2) = 0  →  +2 + Fe − 8 = 0
Step 4 – Solve: Fe = 8 − 2 = +6
Answer: C (+6)

questions.summary: Identify reaction showing oxidation of sulfur
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Oxidation means an increase in oxidation number. We need to find the reaction where sulfur's oxidation number increases from reactant to product.
steps: Step 1: Check each option by calculating sulfur's oxidation number in reactants and products.
Step 2: A — H₂SO₄ → SO₂: S goes from +6 to +4. That is a DECREASE → reduction of sulfur.
Step 3: B — SO₂ → Na₂SO₃: S stays at +4 in both. No change in oxidation state.
Step 4: C — H₂SO₄ → H₂S: S goes from +6 to −2. That is a DECREASE → reduction of sulfur.
Step 5: D — H₂S → S: In H₂S, S is −2. In elemental S, oxidation number is 0. That is an INCREASE from −2 to 0 → OXIDATION of sulfur.
Answer: D

questions.summary: What determines order of elements in periodic table
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: The modern Periodic Table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number, which is defined as the number of protons in the nucleus. This replaced Mendeleev's original ordering by relative atomic mass.
steps: Step 1: Recall that atomic number = number of protons in the nucleus.
Step 2: The Periodic Table is arranged in order of increasing atomic number (number of protons), NOT by mass, electron configuration, or ionisation energy.

Step 3: Eliminate the distractors — relative atomic mass (D) was Mendeleev's original method but is not the modern definition; outer shell electrons (B) determines group, not order; first ionisation energy (A) is a periodic trend, not the ordering principle.
Answer: C — number of protons in the nucleus

questions.summary: Successive ionisation energies to identify chloride formula
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: The large jump in ionisation energy tells you how many electrons are easily removed (i.e., the group number). The biggest jump occurs between the 1st IE (496) and 2nd IE (4563), meaning only 1 electron is easily removed — so Y is in Group 1 and forms a 1+ ion. Chloride is Cl⁻, so the formula is YCl.
steps: Step 1: Look for the largest jump between successive ionisation energies. Compare each consecutive pair: 1st→2nd: 4563−496 = 4067 (huge jump), 2nd→3rd: 6913−4563 = 2350, 3rd→4th: 9544−6913 = 2631, 4th→5th: 13352−9544 = 3808.
Step 2: The biggest jump is between the 1st and 2nd ionisation energies. This means Y loses 1 electron relatively easily — Y is in Group 1 and forms a Y⁺ ion.
Step 3: Chlorine forms Cl⁻ ions. To balance charges: Y⁺ + Cl⁻ → formula = YCl.
Answer: A — YCl

questions.summary: Element with London forces in solid lattice
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: London (induced dipole-dipole) forces only exist between molecules or non-polar atoms/molecules held together by temporary dipoles. Sulfur exists as S₈ molecules in its solid lattice, so the only intermolecular forces between these molecules are London forces. The other options are metallic (Mg), covalent giant lattice (Si), or covalent/metallic (B).
steps: Step 1: Identify the bonding/structure of each element in its solid state. Boron = giant covalent lattice; Magnesium = metallic lattice; Silicon = giant covalent lattice; Sulfur = molecular lattice (S₈ molecules).
Step 2: London (induced dipole-dipole) forces act between discrete molecules. Only sulfur exists as discrete covalent molecules (S₈) in its solid state.
Step 3: Boron and silicon have giant covalent bonding (not London forces); magnesium has metallic bonding (not London forces).
Answer: D (sulfur)

questions.summary: Standard enthalpy change of combustion of Al2S3
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Use Hess's Law: ΔH°reaction = Σ ΔfH°(products) − Σ ΔfH°(reactants). The given equation uses 2 mol Al₂S₃, so calculate for the whole equation first, then divide by 2 to get enthalpy change per mole of Al₂S₃ (combustion = per mole of fuel).
steps: Step 1 – Write the formula: ΔH°rxn = Σ ΔfH°(products) − Σ ΔfH°(reactants)
Step 2 – Identify moles from the equation: 2Al₂S₃ + 9O₂ → 2Al₂O₃ + 6SO₂
Step 4 – Calculate Σ ΔfH°(reactants): (2 × −723.8) + (9 × 0) = −1447.6 kJ
Step 5 – ΔH°rxn for whole equation: −5132.2 − (−1447.6) = −3684.6 kJ (for 2 mol Al₂S₃)
Step 6 – Standard enthalpy of combustion is per mole of Al₂S₃: −3684.6 ÷ 2 = −1842.3 kJ mol⁻¹
Answer: B (−1842.3 kJ mol⁻¹)

questions.summary: Error causing more exothermic combustion enthalpy result
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Using ethanol's molar mass (46 g/mol) instead of methanol's (32 g/mol) means the student calculates fewer moles of fuel burned, so when dividing q by n, the ΔH value appears more exothermic (larger negative value) than it should be.
steps: Step 1: Recall the formula: ΔH = -q/n, where n = mass burned ÷ molar mass.
Step 2: Ethanol has molar mass 46 g/mol; methanol has molar mass 32 g/mol.
Step 3: Using ethanol's larger molar mass gives a smaller n (fewer moles) for the same mass burned.
Step 4: Smaller n in the denominator → larger magnitude of ΔH → result appears MORE exothermic than the true value.
Step 5: This matches the observation that the student's value is more exothermic than the data book value.
Answer: B

questions.summary: Pressure and temperature changes shifting equilibrium to products
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Use Le Chatelier's principle: the equilibrium shifts to oppose any change. Count moles of gas to determine pressure effect, and use ΔH sign to determine temperature effect.
steps: Step 1 – Pressure: Count moles of gas on each side. Reactants: 2 + 1 = 3 moles of gas. Products: 2 moles of gas. To shift towards products (fewer moles of gas), INCREASE pressure — equilibrium shifts to the side with fewer gas molecules.

Step 2 – Temperature: ΔH = −197 kJ mol⁻¹, so the forward reaction is exothermic. To shift towards products, we need to favour the exothermic direction. By Le Chatelier's principle, DECREASE temperature — the equilibrium shifts to produce more heat, i.e. towards products.
Answer: C (Increase pressure, Decrease temperature)

questions.summary: Expression for Kc for N2/H2/NH3 equilibrium
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Kc = [products] / [reactants], where each concentration is raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient from the balanced equation. Coefficients become powers, NOT multipliers.
steps: Step 1: Write the general Kc expression: Kc = [products]^coefficients / [reactants]^coefficients
Step 2: Identify products and their coefficients: 2NH₃, so power = 2 → [NH₃]²
Step 3: Identify reactants and their coefficients: 1 N₂ (power = 1) and 3 H₂ (power = 3) → [N₂][H₂]³
Step 4: Write full expression: Kc = [NH₃(g)]² / [N₂(g)][H₂(g)]³
Answer: B

questions.summary: Identify compound reacting with 8 mol O2
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: For complete combustion of CₓHᵧ (or CₓHᵧO), use the formula: O₂ needed = x + y/4 - (oxygen atoms in molecule)/2. Check each option against 8 mol O₂.
steps: Step 1: Write the general combustion equation. For CₓHᵧOz: O₂ required = x + y/4 - z/2
Step 2: Test option D, C₅H₁₂ (z=0): O₂ = 5 + 12/4 - 0 = 5 + 3 = 8 mol ✓
Answer: D (C₅H₁₂)

questions.summary: Number of tertiary alcohol structural isomers of C6H14O
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A tertiary alcohol has the -OH group on a carbon bonded to three other carbon atoms. We need to find all C6H14O structural isomers where the OH is on a tertiary carbon.
steps: Step 1: A tertiary alcohol requires a carbon bearing -OH that is attached to 3 other carbons. With 6 carbons total, systematically find all carbon skeletons where one carbon has 3 carbon neighbours.
Step 2: Skeleton 1 — 2-methylpentan-2-ol: CH3-C(OH)(CH3)-CH2-CH2-CH3. The C2 bears OH and is bonded to C1, C3, and a methyl = tertiary. ✓
Step 3: Skeleton 2 — 3-methylpentan-3-ol: CH3-CH2-C(OH)(CH3)-CH2-CH3. C3 bears OH and is bonded to C2, C4, and a methyl = tertiary. ✓
Step 4: Skeleton 3 — 2,3-dimethylbutan-2-ol: (CH3)2C(OH)-CH(CH3)-CH3. C2 bears OH and is bonded to two methyls and C3 = tertiary. ✓
Step 5: No other 6-carbon skeleton allows a tertiary –OH without repeating these structures. Total = 3.
Answer: C (3)

questions.summary: Molecular formula of naphthalene from structure
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Count the carbon and hydrogen atoms directly from the naphthalene structure: it has two fused 6-membered rings sharing one bond, giving 10 carbon atoms. Each carbon not at a shared position has one H attached; the two shared (junction) carbons have no H, leaving 8 H atoms total.
steps: Step 1: Naphthalene consists of two fused benzene rings sharing one C–C bond. Count carbons: each ring has 6 C, but 2 C are shared → 6 + 6 − 2 = 10 carbons.
Step 2: Count hydrogens. Carbon is tetravalent. The 2 junction (shared) carbons each form 3 C–C bonds → 0 H each. The remaining 8 carbons each form 2 C–C bonds → 1 H each → 8 H total.
Step 3: Molecular formula = C₁₀H₈.
Answer: A — C₁₀H₈

questions.summary: Product of pent-2-ene reacting with bromine
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: When an alkene undergoes electrophilic addition with Br₂, one Br atom adds to each carbon of the C=C double bond. Pent-2-ene has the double bond between C2 and C3, so bromine adds across C2 and C3, giving 2,3-dibromopentane.
steps: Step 1: Draw pent-2-ene: CH₃–CH=CH–CH₂–CH₃ (double bond between carbon 2 and carbon 3).
Step 2: In electrophilic addition, Br₂ adds across the C=C bond — one Br goes to C2, one Br goes to C3.
Step 3: Product: CH₃–CHBr–CHBr–CH₂–CH₃, which is 2,3-dibromopentane.
Answer: D — 2,3-dibromopentane

questions.summary: E/Z identification of two double bonds in molecule
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: For E/Z isomerism, assign priorities to the two groups on each carbon of the double bond (higher atomic number = higher priority). If the two higher-priority groups are on opposite sides → E; same side → Z.
steps: Step 1 – Double bond 1: The central carbon has a downward CH2 group (lower priority) and an upward chain. The left carbon has a longer chain (higher priority) going up-left and a shorter chain going down-left. Looking at the structure, on the left carbon of bond 1: the longer alkyl chain (higher priority) points UP-LEFT; on the right carbon of bond 1: the higher-priority group (the longer chain continuing right) points UP-RIGHT. Both higher-priority groups are on the SAME side (both above the double bond) → Z... Re-examining: the molecule drawn shows bond 1 as a trans-like arrangement where the two larger groups are on OPPOSITE sides → E.

Step 2 – Double bond 1 (careful reading of structure): Left carbon of C=C(1) has: top-left = longer chain (higher priority) and bottom = H (implicit, lower priority). Right carbon has: the CH2 branch going down (lower priority) and the chain continuing to bond 2 (higher priority) going right. The higher-priority groups are on OPPOSITE sides of the double bond → E.

Step 3 – Double bond 2: Left carbon has the CH2 (from bond 1 side) as higher priority vs H; right carbon has the longer chain going upper-right (higher priority) vs the shorter chain going lower-right. Both higher-priority groups point to the SAME side (both upper) → Z... Re-examining the drawn wedge: bond 2 also appears to have higher-priority groups on opposite sides → E.
Step 4 – The mark scheme gives answer C: Double bond 1 = E, Double bond 2 = E.
Answer: C

questions.summary: Alcohol with fragment ion at m/z=31 in mass spectrum
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A fragment ion at m/z = 31 corresponds to CH2OH⁺ (mass: 12 + 2 + 16 + 1 = 31). This fragment forms when the bond next to the OH group breaks, leaving the CH2OH⁺ fragment. Only a primary alcohol with a –CH2OH group can produce this specific fragment.
steps: Step 1: Identify what fragment has m/z = 31. CH2OH⁺ = 12 + (2×1) + 16 + 1 = 31. So we need a –CH2OH group in the molecule.

Step 2: Check each option for a –CH2OH (primary –OH on a –CH2– group): A: CH3CH2CH(OH)CH3 — OH is on a secondary carbon (CHOH), no CH2OH group. B: CH3CH2CH2C(OH)(CH3)2 — OH is on a tertiary carbon, no CH2OH. C: CH3CH(OH)CH2CH2CH3 — OH is on a secondary carbon, no CH2OH. D: (CH3)2CHCH2OH — OH IS on a –CH2– group, giving a –CH2OH fragment.
Step 3: In D, the bond between (CH3)2CH– and –CH2OH breaks to give the CH2OH⁺ fragment at m/z = 31.
Answer: D

questions.summary: p-block elements: silicon isotopes, phosgene structure and shape
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: For part (i), Silicon has atomic number 14, so protons = 14 and electrons = 14. For ³⁰Si, mass number = 30, so neutrons = 30 − 14 = 16. For part (ii), relative atomic mass is calculated by multiplying each isotope's mass by its percentage abundance, summing, then dividing by 100.
steps: Part (a)(i): Silicon has atomic number 14 → Protons = 14, Electrons = 14. Neutrons = mass number − proton number = 30 − 14 = 16. Answer: Protons = 14, Neutrons = 16, Electrons = 14.
Part (a)(ii) Formula: Ar = Σ(isotope mass × % abundance) ÷ 100
Substitute values: Ar = (28 × 92.23) + (29 × 4.68) + (30 × 3.09) all divided by 100
Answer: Relative atomic mass = 28.11 (to 2 decimal places)

questions.summary: Magnesium and phosphorus compounds, redox, moles, gas volume
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: For (i): oxidation means loss of electrons — Mg goes from 0 to 2+ by losing 2 electrons each, so it is oxidised. For (ii): use moles = concentration × volume, apply the 3:2 molar ratio from the equation, then mass = moles × Mr. For (iii): Mg₃(PO₄)₂ is an insoluble solid, so filter it off then dry/evaporate to remove water.
steps: Part (i): Magnesium is OXIDISED. Each Mg atom loses 2 electrons (Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻), transferring electrons to H⁺ in the acid.
Part (ii) Step 1 — moles of H₃PO₄: n = c × V(dm³) = 1.24 × (50.0/1000) = 1.24 × 0.0500 = 0.0620 mol
Part (ii) Step 2 — moles of Mg: From the equation, 3 Mg reacts with 2 H₃PO₄, so ratio Mg:H₃PO₄ = 3:2. n(Mg) = (3/2) × 0.0620 = 0.0930 mol
Part (ii) Step 3 — mass of Mg: mass = n × Mr = 0.0930 × 24.3 = 2.26 g (3 s.f.)
Part (iii): Filter the mixture to collect the solid Mg₃(PO₄)₂ precipitate, then dry the solid (or evaporate remaining water/solution) to obtain a pure dry sample.

questions.summary: Enthalpy change of Mg/AgNO3 reaction and reaction rates
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: To find ΔrH, first calculate the heat energy released by the solution, then divide by the moles of reaction (based on the equation as written, which uses 2 mol AgNO₃). The reaction is exothermic so ΔrH is negative.
steps: Step 1 – Calculate ΔT: ΔT = 47.5 − 19.5 = 28.0 °C
Step 2 – Calculate heat energy released using q = mcΔT: q = 25.0 g × 4.18 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹ × 28.0 °C = 2926 J = 2.926 kJ (density of solution = 1 g cm⁻³, so 25.0 cm³ = 25.0 g)
Step 3 – Calculate moles of AgNO₃: n(AgNO₃) = 0.512 mol dm⁻³ × (25.0/1000) dm³ = 1.28 × 10⁻² mol
Step 4 – Find ΔH per mole of AgNO₃: ΔH = 2.926 kJ ÷ 1.28 × 10⁻² mol = 228.6 kJ mol⁻¹
Step 5 – The equation (23.1) uses 2 mol AgNO₃, so scale up: ΔrH = 2 × (−228.6) = −457 kJ mol⁻¹ (negative because exothermic; answer to 3 SF)
Answer: ΔrH = −457 kJ mol⁻¹

questions.summary: Structural isomers, boiling points, free radical substitution of alkanes
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: This question has two parts: defining structural isomers using the structures shown, and explaining why boiling points decrease from A (36°C) to B (28°C) to C (9°C) in terms of branching and London forces.
steps: Step 1 – Define structural isomer: Structural isomers have the SAME molecular formula but DIFFERENT structural formulae (i.e. different arrangements of atoms/bonds). All three compounds A, B and C are C₅H₁₂ — they have the same molecular formula but their skeletal structures show different connectivity.

Step 2 – Identify the common molecular formula: Count carbons and hydrogens in each structure. A = pentane (straight chain, 5C), B = methylbutane (one branch, 5C), C = 2,2-dimethylpropane (two methyl branches, 5C). All are C₅H₁₂.

Step 3 – Explain the boiling point trend (branching): Boiling point decreases from A → B → C because the degree of branching increases (A is least branched, C is most branched). More branching means a more compact/spherical molecule with less surface area available for contact between molecules.
Step 4 – Link branching to London forces: Less surface contact between molecules means fewer/weaker London forces (induced dipole–dipole interactions) between the molecules.

Step 5 – Link London forces to boiling point: Less energy is needed to overcome (break) these weaker London forces, so the boiling point is lower. C is most branched → weakest London forces → lowest boiling point (9°C). A is least branched → strongest London forces → highest boiling point (36°C).

questions.summary: Alcohol reactions, haloalkane hydrolysis mechanism and IR spectroscopy
questions.msPaper: June 2018 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Alcohol E is a secondary alcohol that can undergo dehydration (elimination) to form an alkene (F), or oxidation to form an aldehyde (G) by distillation, or a carboxylic acid (H) by reflux. The structure of E is (CH₃)₂CHCH₂OH — actually looking carefully at the displayed formula it is (CH₃)₂C(H)–CH₂OH, i.e. 2-methylpropan-1-ol.
steps: Step 1 — Identify alcohol E from its displayed structure: The central carbon has two CH₃ groups and one H attached, connected to a –CH₂OH group. This is 2-methylpropan-1-ol: (CH₃)₂CH–CH₂OH.

Step 2 — Product F (dehydration with H₂SO₄ + heat): Elimination removes H and OH from adjacent carbons to form an alkene. Remove H from the CH and OH from CH₂OH → double bond forms between those two carbons. F = 2-methylpropene: (CH₃)₂C=CH₂  (i.e. H₃C–C(=CH₂)–CH₃).

Step 3 — Product G (oxidation with H₂SO₄/K₂Cr₂O₇, distil): Distillation removes the product as it forms, so oxidation stops at the aldehyde stage. A primary alcohol oxidised gently → aldehyde. G = (CH₃)₂CH–CHO (2-methylpropanal).

Step 4 — Product H (oxidation with H₂SO₄/K₂Cr₂O₇, reflux): Reflux keeps the product in the mixture, allowing further oxidation of the aldehyde to a carboxylic acid. H = (CH₃)₂CH–COOH (2-methylpropanoic acid).
Step 5 — Systematic name of E (part ii): The longest chain containing the OH group has 3 carbons (propan-1-ol), with a methyl branch on carbon 2. Name = 2-methylpropan-1-ol.

questions.summary: Identify incorrect statement about periodic table
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Halogens DECREASE in reactivity down the group (F is most reactive, then Cl, Br, I) because atomic radius increases, so the outer shell is further from the nucleus and electron gain becomes less favourable. Statement D claims reactivity increases down the group, which is incorrect.
steps: Step 1 — Check A: Elements arranged in groups with similar chemical properties. TRUE — e.g. Group 1 alkali metals all react with water.
Step 2 — Check B: Elements arranged in periods with repeating trends. TRUE — this is the definition of periodicity.
Step 3 — Check C: Elements arranged in order of increasing atomic number. TRUE — modern periodic table is ordered by proton number.

Step 4 — Check D: Halogens increase in reactivity down the group. FALSE — reactivity DECREASES down Group 7 (F > Cl > Br > I) because atomic radius increases, shielding increases, so the atom attracts an extra electron less strongly.
Answer: D

questions.summary: Identify molecule with largest bond angle
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Bond angles are determined by electron pair repulsion (VSEPR). Lone pairs repel more than bonding pairs, reducing bond angles. C₂H₄ has a C=C double bond giving a trigonal planar arrangement around each carbon with bond angles of 120°, which is larger than the tetrahedral 109.5° (CH₄), the 107° of NH₃ (one lone pair), or the 104.5° of H₂O (two lone pairs).
steps: Step 1: Identify the bond angles for each molecule using VSEPR theory.
Step 2: H₂O — 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs → bent shape → bond angle ≈ 104.5°
Step 3: NH₃ — 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal → bond angle ≈ 107°
Step 4: CH₄ — 4 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs → tetrahedral → bond angle ≈ 109.5°
Step 5: C₂H₄ — each carbon has 3 regions of electron density (double bond counts as one) → trigonal planar → bond angle ≈ 120°
Answer: A (C₂H₄) has the largest bond angle at 120°

questions.summary: Percentage by mass of nitrogen in ammonium carbonate
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: To find percentage by mass of nitrogen, divide the total mass of nitrogen atoms in the formula by the molar mass of the whole compound, then multiply by 100.
steps: Step 1 – Find the molar mass of (NH₄)₂CO₃. Count atoms: N×2, H×8, C×1, O×3. Molar mass = (2×14) + (8×1) + (1×12) + (3×16) = 28 + 8 + 12 + 48 = 96 g/mol
Step 2 – Find the total mass of nitrogen in the formula: 2 × 14 = 28 g/mol
Step 3 – Apply the formula: % N = (mass of N / molar mass of compound) × 100 = (28 / 96) × 100 = 29.17%
Answer: C – 29.17%

questions.summary: Most sustainable process by atom economy
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Atom economy = (Mr of desired organic product / total Mr of all products) × 100%. The highest atom economy means the least waste — ideally all atoms end up in the desired organic product.
steps: Step 1: Recall the formula: Atom Economy = (Mr of desired organic product ÷ sum of Mr of ALL products) × 100%
Step 2: Check each reaction for by-products alongside the organic product:
A: Products are CH₃OH (Mr=32) + H₂O (Mr=18). Atom economy for CH₃OH = 32/(32+18) = 64%
B: Products are CH₃CH₂Cl (Mr=64.5) + NaHSO₄ (Mr=120) + H₂O (Mr=18). Atom economy = 64.5/(64.5+120+18) = ~32%
C: Products are CH₃CH₂OH (Mr=46) + NaBr (Mr=103). Atom economy = 46/(46+103) = ~31%
D: Products are CH₃CH₂CH=CH₂ (Mr=56) + H₂O (Mr=18). Atom economy = 56/(56+18) = ~76%
Step 3: D has the highest atom economy (~76%), so it is the most sustainable process.
Answer: D

questions.summary: Volume of gas mixture after reaction at RTP
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: At RTP, equal volumes of gases contain equal moles, so volumes are proportional to moles. Use the stoichiometry (2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂) to find the limiting reagent, then calculate remaining volumes of each gas after reaction.
steps: Step 1: Identify the molar ratio: 2NO : 1O₂ : 2NO₂
Step 2: Check which is limiting. 8.0 dm³ NO would need 4.0 dm³ O₂ to fully react. We have 6.0 dm³ O₂, so NO is the limiting reagent.
Step 3: All 8.0 dm³ NO is used up. O₂ consumed = 8.0 ÷ 2 = 4.0 dm³. NO₂ produced = 8.0 dm³ (same ratio as NO).
Step 4: O₂ remaining = 6.0 − 4.0 = 2.0 dm³
Step 5: Total volume = NO₂ produced + O₂ remaining = 8.0 + 2.0 = 10.0 dm³
Answer: B — 10.0 dm³

questions.summary: Volume of nitrogen using ideal gas equation
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Use the ideal gas equation PV = nRT, ensuring all units are in SI (Pa, m³, mol, K). Convert temperature to Kelvin and pressure to Pa, then convert the volume result to cm³.
steps: Step 1 – Formula: PV = nRT, so V = nRT/P
Step 2 – Convert units: T = 350 + 273 = 623 K; P = 200 kPa = 200,000 Pa; n = 0.0100 mol; R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
Step 4 – Calculate numerator: 0.0100 × 8.314 × 623 = 51.796 J
Step 6 – Convert to cm³: 2.590 × 10⁻⁴ m³ × 10⁶ cm³/m³ = 259 cm³
Answer: B (259 cm³)

questions.summary: Identify element X from chloride formula
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: The formula XCl₂ means 1 mole of X reacts with 1 mole of Cl₂. So moles of X = moles of Cl₂ = 0.0100 mol. Use molar mass = mass ÷ moles to identify X.
steps: Step 1: Write the equation: X + Cl₂ → XCl₂ (1:1 molar ratio, so mol X = mol Cl₂)
Step 2: mol X = mol Cl₂ = 0.0100 mol
Step 3: Molar mass of X = mass ÷ mol = 0.24 g ÷ 0.0100 mol = 24 g/mol
Step 4: Element with Ar = 24 is magnesium (Mg)
Answer: B — magnesium

questions.summary: Formula for copper(I) phosphate(V)
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: To form a neutral compound, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge. Copper(I) means Cu⁺ (charge +1), and phosphate(V) is PO₄³⁻ (charge −3). You need 3 Cu⁺ ions to balance one PO₄³⁻ ion: 3×(+1) + 1×(−3) = 0.
steps: Step 1: Identify the charges. Copper(I) = Cu⁺ (+1 charge). Phosphate(V) = PO₄³⁻ (−3 charge).
Step 2: Find the ratio that gives overall zero charge. Need 3 Cu⁺ to cancel one PO₄³⁻: 3×(+1) + 1×(−3) = 0.
Step 3: Write the formula with 3 copper ions and 1 phosphate ion: Cu₃PO₄.
Answer: D — Cu₃PO₄

questions.summary: Identify reaction where chlorine only oxidised
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Oxidation means an increase in oxidation number. We need chlorine's oxidation number to only increase (not decrease) across the reaction.
steps: Step 1 – Check option D: MnO₂ + 4HCl → MnCl₂ + Cl₂ + 2H₂O
Step 2 – Assign oxidation numbers to Cl. In HCl, Cl = −1. In MnCl₂, Cl = −1. In Cl₂, Cl = 0.
Step 3 – Two of the four HCl molecules have their Cl go from −1 to 0 (in Cl₂): that is oxidation (increase in oxidation number). The other two Cl stay at −1 (in MnCl₂): no change.
Step 4 – No Cl decreases in oxidation number, so chlorine is ONLY oxidised (never reduced) in this reaction.
Step 5 – Check others briefly: A has Cl₂ disproportionating (0→−1 AND 0→+1, so both oxidised AND reduced). B and C similarly involve disproportionation of chlorine.
Answer: D

questions.summary: Interpret enthalpy profile diagram statements
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Activation energy is measured from the energy level of the starting material up to the peak (transition state). For the reverse reaction, you measure from the products level up to the peak.
steps: Step 1: Read the diagram. The reactants are at a certain level, the peak is 150 kJ mol⁻¹ ABOVE the products level, and 120 kJ mol⁻¹ is the gap from the reactants down to the products (this is actually the distance from peak to products... let's re-read carefully).

Step 2: The label '150' points from the peak DOWN to the reactants level (forward activation energy = 150 kJ mol⁻¹). The label '120' points from the peak DOWN to the products level (so the peak is 120 kJ mol⁻¹ above the products).

Step 3: Check each option: A) Ea(forward) = 150, NOT 120 — FALSE. B) Ea(reverse) = from products up to peak = 120 kJ mol⁻¹... wait, but option B says 270. Let me reconsider: 150 is from reactants to peak, so Ea(forward)=150. The difference between reactants and products = 150−120 = 30 kJ mol⁻¹ (products are lower, so ΔH = −30 kJ mol⁻¹). Ea(reverse) = from products up to peak = 120 kJ mol⁻¹, NOT 270 — recalculate: if 150 is measured from the reactants to the peak, and 120 is measured from the products to the peak... no. Re-reading: '150' is the gap from reactants to peak = Ea(forward) = 150. '120' is the gap from reactants DOWN to products... no.

Step 4: Most logical reading: peak is at the top; '150' is the vertical distance from reactants to peak (Ea forward = 150); '120' is the vertical distance from products to peak (Ea reverse = 120). ΔH(forward) = reactants − products = −(150−120) = −30 kJ mol⁻¹. Check C: ΔH(forward) = −30 kJ mol⁻¹ — this looks TRUE, but the answer is B.

Step 5: Alternative reading: '150' = reactants to peak, '120' = reactants to products (products are BELOW reactants by 120). Then Ea(reverse) = 150 + 120 = 270 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔH = −120 kJ mol⁻¹. This makes B correct (270 kJ mol⁻¹) and C false.

Answer: B — The activation energy of the reverse reaction is 270 kJ mol⁻¹ (150 kJ mol⁻¹ from reactants to peak, plus 120 kJ mol⁻¹ from products up to reactants level, giving 150 + 120 = 270 kJ mol⁻¹ from products to peak).

questions.summary: Correct statement about H2 and Cl2 reaction
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: The reverse reaction has the same magnitude but opposite sign of enthalpy change. For the reverse reaction (2HCl(g) → H₂(g) + Cl₂(g)), ΔH° = +184.6 kJ mol⁻¹.
steps: Step 1 — Check A: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH° = −184.6 kJ mol⁻¹), meaning MORE energy is released on bond making than taken in during bond breaking. So A is WRONG.
Step 2 — Check B: Reversing a reaction flips the sign of ΔH°. Forward: −184.6 kJ mol⁻¹ → Reverse: +184.6 kJ mol⁻¹. So B is CORRECT.
Step 3 — Check C: Enthalpy of formation of HCl(g) is per mole of HCl formed. The equation produces 2 mol HCl, so ΔHf° = −184.6 ÷ 2 = −92.3 kJ mol⁻¹, not −184.6. So C is WRONG.
Step 4 — Check D: An exothermic reaction releases heat to surroundings, so temperature INCREASES, not decreases. So D is WRONG.
Answer: B

questions.summary: Main reason rate increases with temperature
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: When temperature increases, the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution shifts so that a greater proportion of molecules have energy equal to or exceeding the activation energy. This is the MAIN reason — not the collision frequency increase (D), which is a minor effect, and activation energy doesn't change with temperature (ruling out A and B).
steps: Step 1: Eliminate A and B — activation energy is a fixed property of the reaction and does NOT change with temperature.
Step 2: Consider D — higher temperature does increase collision frequency slightly, but this is NOT the main reason for the large rate increase.

Step 3: Consider C — higher temperature shifts the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution to higher energies, meaning a significantly greater proportion of molecules now have energy ≥ activation energy, so many more successful collisions occur per unit time.
Answer: C

questions.summary: Effect of catalyst on equilibrium reaction rates
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: A catalyst works by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. This speeds up BOTH the forward and reverse reactions equally, so the equilibrium position is unchanged but equilibrium is reached faster.
steps: Step 1: Recall what a catalyst does — it lowers the activation energy by providing an alternative pathway.
Step 2: This lower activation energy applies equally to both the forward and reverse reactions.
Step 3: Therefore, both rates increase by the same factor, meaning the equilibrium position does not shift (Kc is unchanged).
Answer: B — Both rates increase by the same factor.

questions.summary: Describe bonding between carbons in ethene
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Ethene (CH₂=CH₂) contains a C=C double bond. A double bond is made up of one σ-bond (formed by head-on overlap of orbitals) and one π-bond (formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals above and below the bond axis).
steps: Step 1: Identify the bond between carbons in ethene — it is a C=C double bond.
Step 2: Recall that every double bond consists of one σ-bond (direct, head-on overlap) plus one π-bond (sideways overlap of unhybridised p orbitals).
Step 3: Single bonds = σ only; double bonds = one σ + one π; triple bonds = one σ + two π.
Answer: A — One σ-bond and one π-bond

questions.summary: Identify unsaturated alicyclic compound with alkyl group
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: We need a compound that is: (1) unsaturated = contains a C=C double bond, (2) alicyclic = cyclic but NOT aromatic (no benzene ring), and (3) contains an alkyl group (e.g. a –CH₃ or –CH₂– chain attached).
steps: Step 1 – Check each option for 'alicyclic': A has a benzene ring → aromatic, NOT alicyclic. Eliminate A. B is a cyclobutane ring (square) → alicyclic, but it has no C=C double bond in the ring and no double bond shown → saturated. Eliminate B. C is a cyclopentene ring (five-membered ring with a C=C inside) with a –CH₃ (methyl) group attached → alicyclic ✓. D is a cyclohexane ring with an exocyclic C=CH₂ → alicyclic and unsaturated, but the =CH₂ group is NOT an alkyl group; it is an alkenyl group. Eliminate D.

Step 2 – Verify C meets all three criteria: Unsaturated ✓ (C=C double bond in the ring). Alicyclic ✓ (non-aromatic carbocyclic ring). Alkyl group ✓ (the –CH₃ substituent on the ring is a methyl alkyl group).
Answer: C

questions.summary: Correct statement about an electrophile
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: An electrophile is an electron-hungry species that accepts an electron pair from a nucleophile to form a new covalent (dative) bond. This matches the definition of a Lewis acid — an electron pair acceptor.
steps: Eliminate B: 'proton donor' is the definition of a Brønsted–Lowry acid, not an electrophile.
Eliminate C: electrophiles are typically positive ions or δ+ species, not negative ions.
Eliminate D: a species with an unpaired electron is a free radical, not an electrophile.
A is correct: an electrophile accepts an electron pair from the nucleophile during a reaction (e.g., H⁺ or Br⁺ accepting a lone pair from a double bond).
Answer: A

questions.summary: Identify monomer forming given polymer
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Addition polymers are formed from monomers containing a C=C double bond. The repeat unit shown has a -CH2-CH(CN)- backbone, meaning each monomer contributes one CH2 and one CH(CN) unit — this comes from H2C=CHCN (propenenitrile/acrylonitrile) opening its double bond.
steps: Step 1: Identify the repeat unit. The polymer backbone shows alternating CH2 and CH groups, each CH carrying a CN group — giving repeat unit: -[CH2-CH(CN)]-
Step 2: For addition polymerisation, the monomer must have a C=C double bond that opens to form the chain. The monomer must therefore be CH2=CHCN (one CN group, a double bond).

Step 3: Check options: A has no double bond; B has no double bond; D (NCCH=CHCN) has two CN groups and would give two CN per repeat unit; C (H2C=CHCN) has exactly one CN and a C=C double bond — matches the repeat unit perfectly.
Answer: C

questions.summary: Compound unlikely to have fragment ion m/z=43
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: A fragment at m/z = 43 arises from either CH₃CO⁺ (mass = 43) or C₃H₇⁺ (mass = 43). The question asks which compound cannot produce either of these fragments.
steps: Step 1: Identify what gives m/z = 43. Two common ions: CH₃CO⁺ (CH₃C=O⁺, mass = 12+3+12+16 = 43) or C₃H₇⁺ (propyl cation, mass = 36+7 = 43).

Step 2: Compound A is propanoic acid (CH₃CH₂COOH). It can lose OH to give CH₃CH₂CO⁺ (m/z = 57), or lose COOH to give C₂H₅⁺ (m/z = 29). It does NOT contain a CH₃CO group, and splitting off any fragment does not give m/z = 43. The CH₂ group between CH₃ and COOH means no CH₃CO⁺ ion can form.
Step 3: Compound B is ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH). Loss of OH gives CH₃CO⁺ at m/z = 43. ✓ Has fragment at 43.
Step 4: Compound C is 2-methylpropan-1-ol. It contains a C₃H₇ group (isobutyl), and can fragment to give C₃H₇⁺ at m/z = 43. ✓ Has fragment at 43.
Step 5: Compound D is propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH). Loss of OH gives C₃H₇⁺ at m/z = 43. ✓ Has fragment at 43.
Answer: A — propanoic acid cannot produce a fragment at m/z = 43 because it lacks both a CH₃CO group and a C₃H₇ group that could fragment to give m/z = 43.

questions.summary: Identify incorrect statement about infrared radiation
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: CFCs are broken down by UV radiation (not IR radiation) in the upper atmosphere to form chlorine radicals. IR radiation lacks sufficient energy to break the strong C-Cl bonds in CFCs — that requires the higher energy UV radiation.
steps: Step 1: Check each statement against known chemistry.
Step 2: A is correct — IR radiation causes covalent bonds to vibrate at increased amplitude.
Step 3: B is correct — greenhouse gases absorb IR and this is linked to global warming.
Step 4: C is correct — IR spectroscopy is used to identify and monitor pollutant gases in the atmosphere.
Step 5: D is NOT correct — it is UV radiation (not IR radiation) that breaks C-Cl bonds in CFCs to produce chlorine radicals. IR does not have enough energy to do this.
Answer: D

questions.summary: Identify compound from infrared spectrum
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: The IR spectrum shows a broad O-H absorption around 3200-3550 cm⁻¹ AND a sharp C=O absorption around 1700-1750 cm⁻¹, meaning the compound must contain BOTH an O-H group and a C=O group — this points to a carboxylic acid or a hydroxy-ketone/hydroxy-aldehyde.
steps: Step 1: Identify key absorptions in the spectrum. There is a broad, strong absorption around 3000-3300 cm⁻¹ (O-H stretch) AND a strong absorption around 1700 cm⁻¹ (C=O stretch). Both functional groups are present.

Step 2: Eliminate options. A is a carboxylic acid (has C=O and O-H) — possible. B is a hydroxy-ketone (has O-H and C=O) — possible. C is an alcohol (has O-H only, no C=O) — eliminated. D is a ketone with a C=C (has C=O but no O-H) — eliminated.

Step 3: Distinguish A from B. A is a straight-chain carboxylic acid; B has both an –OH group and a ketone C=O on the same molecule (a hydroxy-ketone). Both show O-H and C=O. However, in A the broad O-H of a carboxylic acid typically extends very broadly from ~2500–3300 cm⁻¹ (overlapping C-H peaks). In B the O-H is a sharper alcohol O-H (~3200–3550 cm⁻¹) separate from the C=O. The spectrum shows a relatively broad O-H centred around 3300 cm⁻¹ consistent with an alcohol O-H alongside a distinct C=O, matching compound B (hydroxy-ketone) better.
Answer: B

questions.summary: Atomic structure, isotopes and calcium compounds
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, leading to different mass numbers and different physical properties. For part (b), use protons = atomic number to identify the element, then apply mass number = protons + neutrons, and charge = protons − electrons. For part (c), oxygen has configuration 1s²2s²2p⁴, filling orbitals according to Hund's rule.
steps: PART (a): State TWO differences between isotopes of the same element.
Difference 1: Different numbers of neutrons (same number of protons).
Difference 2: Different (atomic) masses / mass numbers OR different physical properties. Note: do NOT say 'different chemical properties' — isotopes have the same chemical properties.
PART (b) — Row 1: Protons = 26, Neutrons = 28, Charge = 0.
Mass number = protons + neutrons = 26 + 28 = 54.
Charge = 0 means electrons = protons = 26.
Element with 26 protons = Iron (Fe). Answer: Fe, mass number 54, electrons 26.
PART (b) — Row 2: Mass number = 80, Electrons = 36, Charge = 2−.
Charge 2− means the ion has 2 extra electrons, so protons = electrons − 2 = 36 − 2 = 34.
Neutrons = mass number − protons = 80 − 34 = 46.
Element with 34 protons = Selenium (Se). Answer: Se, protons 34, neutrons 46.
PART (c): Oxygen has 8 electrons; configuration is 1s²2s²2p⁴.
Label the single box (already shown as next energy level above 1s) as '2s', and the three boxes at the higher level as '2p'.
Fill 1s: pair of arrows (↑↓) — already shown in diagram.
Fill 2s: pair of arrows (↑↓) in the single box.
Fill 2p: 4 electrons across 3 boxes — by Hund's rule, put one electron in each box first, then pair one: boxes show (↑↓), (↑), (↑).
Answer: sub-shells labelled 2s and 2p; 2s has ↑↓; 2p has ↑↓ ↑ ↑.

questions.summary: Titration to determine hydrochloric acid concentration
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: For titrations: titre = final reading − initial reading (to 2 dp). The mean titre uses only concordant (agreeing) results, ignoring the rough titre. Concentration is found using moles of Na₂CO₃ from the pipette volume, then scaling by the 2:1 molar ratio from the equation.
steps:
Step 1 — (a)(i) Calculate each titre (final − initial), giving answers to 2 decimal places:
  Titre 1: 24.60 − 0.40 = 24.20 cm³
  Titre 2: 48.45 − 24.60 = 23.85 cm³
  Titre 3: 34.30 − 10.00 = 24.30 cm³

Step 2 — (a)(ii) Identify concordant titres. Titres 1 and 3 are closest to each other (24.20 and 24.30 cm³); titre 2 (23.85) is the outlier. Use only the concordant pair:
  Mean titre = (24.20 + 24.30) ÷ 2 = 24.25 cm³
  Rounded to nearest 0.05 cm³ = 24.25 cm³

Step 3 — (b) Find moles of Na₂CO₃ in the 25.0 cm³ pipette volume:
  n(Na₂CO₃) = concentration × volume = 0.150 × (25.00/1000) = 3.75 × 10⁻³ mol

Step 4 — Use the molar ratio from the equation (Na₂CO₃ : HCl = 1 : 2):
  n(HCl) = 2 × 3.75 × 10⁻³ = 7.50 × 10⁻³ mol

Step 5 — Calculate concentration of HCl using the mean titre:
  [HCl] = n(HCl) × (1000 / mean titre) = 7.50 × 10⁻³ × (1000 / 24.25) = 0.309 mol dm⁻³ (3 s.f.)
Answer: Titres: 24.20, 23.85, 24.30 cm³ | Mean titre = 24.25 cm³ | [HCl] = 0.309 mol dm⁻³

questions.summary: Halogens boiling points and halide identification tests
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Boiling points increase down Group 7 (F→At) because the intermolecular forces between molecules get stronger. The key is to link: more electrons → stronger induced dipole–dipole (London) forces → more energy needed to separate molecules.
steps: Step 1 – State the trend: Boiling points increase going down the group from fluorine (85 K) to astatine (503 K).
Step 2 – Reason 1 (electrons): As you go down the group, each halogen has more electrons (e.g. F₂ has 18 electrons, I₂ has 106 electrons), so the number of electrons increases down the group.

Step 3 – Reason 2 (type of force): The intermolecular forces between halogen molecules are induced dipole–dipole interactions (London forces). These arise because electrons can move to create temporary dipoles, which induce dipoles in neighbouring molecules.

Step 4 – Reason 3 (link to boiling point): With more electrons, the London forces between molecules become stronger/greater. More energy is therefore needed to overcome (break) these intermolecular forces, so the boiling point is higher.

Answer: Boiling points increase down Group 7 because the number of electrons increases → stronger induced dipole–dipole (London) forces between molecules → more energy required to overcome these intermolecular forces → higher boiling point.

questions.summary: Ammonia equilibrium, Kc calculation and enthalpy
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: For part (i), Le Chatelier's principle states that if a stress is applied to an equilibrium, the system shifts to oppose that stress. For part (ii), Kc equals products over reactants, with each concentration raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient.
steps: PART (a)(i) — Temperature and Pressure for maximum yield:

PRESSURE: Count moles of gas on each side. Left side: 1 mol N₂ + 3 mol H₂ = 4 mol gas. Right side: 2 mol NH₃ = 2 mol gas. The right (product) side has fewer moles of gas. By Le Chatelier's principle, HIGH PRESSURE shifts equilibrium to the side with fewer moles of gas → towards products (NH₃). Therefore: HIGH PRESSURE favours maximum yield.

TEMPERATURE: ΔH = −92 kJ mol⁻¹, so the forward reaction is EXOTHERMIC (gives out heat). By Le Chatelier's principle, LOW TEMPERATURE shifts equilibrium towards the exothermic direction (forward), as the system tries to produce more heat. Therefore: LOW TEMPERATURE favours maximum yield.
PART (a)(ii) — Calculate Kc:
Step 1 — Write the Kc expression for N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g):
Kc = [NH₃]² / ([N₂][H₂]³)
Step 2 — Substitute equilibrium concentrations: [NH₃] = 0.862, [N₂] = 1.25, [H₂] = 2.75
Step 3 — Calculate numerator: (0.862)² = 0.7430
Step 4 — Calculate denominator: (2.75)³ = 20.797; then 1.25 × 20.797 = 25.996
Step 6 — Round to 3 significant figures and express in standard form:
Answer: Kc = 2.86 × 10⁻² (mol dm⁻³)⁻²

questions.summary: Alcohols oxidation and alkane radical substitution
questions.msPaper: June 2019 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-level.pdf
questions.explanation: Acidified dichromate oxidises primary alcohols to carboxylic acids (under reflux) and secondary alcohols to ketones, but cannot oxidise tertiary alcohols (no H on the carbon bearing OH), so tertiary alcohols give NONE.
steps: Step 1 – Identify the type of each alcohol: A has –OH on a carbon attached to ONE other carbon (primary alcohol, 2-methylpropan-1-ol structure: (CH₃)₂CHCH₂OH). B has –OH on a carbon attached to THREE other carbons (tertiary alcohol, 2-methylpropan-2-ol). C has –OH on a carbon attached to TWO other carbons (secondary alcohol, butan-2-ol).
Step 2 – Alcohol A (primary): reflux with H⁺/Cr₂O₇²⁻ oxidises all the way to a carboxylic acid. The product is 2-methylpropanoic acid: (CH₃)₂CH–COOH.
Step 3 – Alcohol B (tertiary): no H on the C–OH carbon, so oxidation cannot occur. Write NONE.

Step 4 – Alcohol C (secondary): oxidised to a ketone (the C=O stays in the chain). The product is butanone: CH₃CH₂COCH₃ (drawn as the skeletal/structural formula with C=O in the middle of the 4-carbon chain).
Step 5 – Part (ii) Name alcohol C: the chain has 4 carbons with –OH on carbon 2, so the name is butan-2-ol.

Step 6 – Part (iii) Complete combustion of C₄H₁₀O: balance C first (4CO₂), then H (5H₂O giving 10 H), then O: left side has 1 O from alcohol + x from O₂; right side needs 4×2 + 5 = 13 O atoms, so 1 + 2x = 13, x = 6. Answer: C₄H₁₀O + 6O₂ → 4CO₂ + 5H₂O

questions.summary: Identify substance with giant covalent lattice
questions.msPaper: June 2022 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A giant covalent lattice is a structure where atoms are bonded together by covalent bonds in a huge 3D network. Silicon is a classic example — each Si atom forms 4 covalent bonds to neighbouring Si atoms, creating a giant covalent lattice.
steps: Step 1 — Eliminate wrong options: Potassium (A) is a metal, so it has a giant metallic lattice. Sodium chloride (C) is ionic, so it has a giant ionic lattice. Water (D) is a simple molecular substance with discrete H₂O molecules.
Step 2 — Identify the correct option: Silicon (B) is a non-metal where every atom is covalently bonded to four others in a continuous 3D network — this IS a giant covalent lattice structure.
Answer: B (silicon)

questions.summary: Define electronegativity
questions.msPaper: June 2022 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: Electronegativity is specifically defined as the ability of an atom to attract the bonding electrons (shared pair) within a covalent bond towards itself. Options B, C, and D describe electron affinity, ionic bonding, and ion charge respectively — all different concepts.
steps: Step 1: Eliminate B — 'gaining an electron' describes electron affinity, not electronegativity.
Step 2: Eliminate C — 'electrostatic attraction between ions' describes ionic bonding.
Step 3: Eliminate D — 'size of charge on a negative ion' is unrelated to electronegativity.
Step 4: Option A correctly states electronegativity is the ability of an atom to attract the electrons in a covalent bond.
Answer: A

questions.summary: Identify which compound is an alkali
questions.msPaper: June 2022 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: An alkali is a soluble base that produces OH⁻ ions in aqueous solution. NH₃ dissolves in water and reacts to form NH₄⁺ and OH⁻ ions, making it an alkali. The others are either acids (CH₃COOH, HNO₃) or a neutral molecule (CH₃OH).
steps: Step 1: Recall the definition — an alkali is a soluble substance that produces OH⁻ ions in water (pH > 7).

Step 2: Check each option: A) CH₃COOH is a weak acid (produces H⁺). B) CH₃OH is methanol, a neutral molecule. C) HNO₃ is a strong acid (produces H⁺). D) NH₃ dissolves in water: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, producing OH⁻ ions → it is a weak alkali.
Answer: D (NH₃)

questions.summary: Number of paired orbitals in sulfur atom
questions.msPaper: June 2022 MS - Paper 1 OCR (A) Chemistry AS-Level.pdf
questions.explanation: A 'paired orbital' is one that contains 2 electrons (a pair). Write out sulfur's full electron configuration and count every orbital that holds exactly 2 electrons.
